Policy language on this driver — CHANGED since the previous statement
  - [date] Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement For immediate release Information received since the Federal Open Market Committee met in October suggests that economic activity has been expanding at a moderate pace.
  - The Committee currently expects that, with gradual adjustments in the stance of monetary policy, economic activity will continue to expand at a moderate pace and labor market indicators will continue to strengthen.
  - Given the economic outlook, and recognizing the time it takes for policy actions to affect future economic outcomes, the Committee decided to raise the target range for the federal funds rate to 1/4 to 1/2 percent.
  - The stance of monetary policy remains accommodative after this increase, thereby supporting further improvement in labor market conditions and a return to 2 percent inflation.
  + The Committee currently expects that, with gradual adjustments in the stance of monetary policy, economic activity will expand at a moderate pace and labor market indicators will continue to strengthen.
  + Given the economic outlook, the Committee decided to maintain the target range for the federal funds rate at 1/4 to 1/2 percent.
  + The stance of monetary policy remains accommodative, thereby supporting further improvement in labor market conditions and a return to 2 percent inflation.

Policy language on this driver — unchanged context
  · Inflation has continued to run below the Committee's 2 percent longer-run objective, partly reflecting declines in energy prices and in prices of non-energy imports.
  · In determining the timing and size of future adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the Committee will assess realized and expected economic conditions relative to its objectives of maximum employment and 2 percent inflation.
  · The Committee expects that economic conditions will evolve in a manner that will warrant only gradual increases in the federal funds rate; the federal funds rate is likely to remain, for some time, below levels that are expected to prevail in the longer run.
  · However, the actual path of the federal funds rate will depend on the economic outlook as informed by incoming data.
  · The Committee is maintaining its existing policy of reinvesting principal payments from its holdings of agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities in agency mortgage-backed securities and of rolling over maturing Treasury securities at auction, and it anticipates doing so until normalization of the level of the federal funds rate is well under way.
  · This policy, by keeping the Committee's holdings of longer-term securities at sizable levels, should help maintain accommodative financial conditions.
  · Voting for the FOMC monetary policy action were: Janet L.

The Committee expects that economic conditions will evolve in a manner that will warrant only gradual increases in the federal funds rate; the federal funds rate is likely to remain, for some time, below levels that are expected to prevail in the longer run.

raw_response: {"report": "The 2s10s spread has been essentially flat-to-modestly range-bound at 1.15-1.19pp over the last 10 readings, with the latest print (1.18) right at the middle of that recent cluster and near the top of its own short-term wobble. Longer-term momentum shows mild flattening pressure (63d change -0.22pp) but the very short-term change (21d -0.05pp) is small, and the last few prints (1.16, 1.18, 1.17, 1.18) show no clear directional trend, just noise. The spread sits only 0.03pp above its 12-month low and in just the 5th percentile of its 12-month range (slope_range_position 0.05), indicating the curve is near its most flattened point of the past year \u2014 but it isn't breaking further to new lows currently, it's stabilizing there. The policy language shift (from a hike to a \"maintain\" statement) reduces near-term front-end upward pressure, which could allow the curve to hold or steepen slightly rather than flatten further. With no fresh hiking signal and the spread hovering near-term without a fresh breakdown, I expect the spread to stay essentially unchanged into month-end, perhaps with a very slight steepening bias given the pause language, but not enough to call a strong directional move.", "key_evidence": ["slope_2s10s last 10 obs", "slope_change_21d", "slope_change_63d", "slope_range_position", "slope_above_12m_low"], "falsifier": "A break below 1.15pp (new 12-month low) or a sustained move above 1.20pp would confirm renewed flattening or steepening respectively.", "missing_inputs": [{"driver": "financial_conditions", "why": "Would clarify if broader tightening/loosening pressures are pushing long-end yields independent of Fed statement language."}, {"driver": "term_premium", "why": "Would show if long-end richness/cheapness is driving the 10s10y move rather than pure policy expectations."}], "direction": "flat", "conviction": 0.35}

view.reasoning: The 2s10s spread has been essentially flat-to-modestly range-bound at 1.15-1.19pp over the last 10 readings, with the latest print (1.18) right at the middle of that recent cluster and near the top of its own short-term wobble. Longer-term momentum shows mild flattening pressure (63d change -0.22pp) but the very short-term change (21d -0.05pp) is small, and the last few prints (1.16, 1.18, 1.17, 1.18) show no clear directional trend, just noise. The spread sits only 0.03pp above its 12-month low and in just the 5th percentile of its 12-month range (slope_range_position 0.05), indicating the curve is near its most flattened point of the past year — but it isn't breaking further to new lows currently, it's stabilizing there. The policy language shift (from a hike to a "maintain" statement) reduces near-term front-end upward pressure, which could allow the curve to hold or steepen slightly rather than flatten further. With no fresh hiking signal and the spread hovering near-term without a fresh breakdown, I expect the spread to stay essentially unchanged into month-end, perhaps with a very slight steepening bias given the pause language, but not enough to call a strong directional move.
view.report: The 2s10s spread has been essentially flat-to-modestly range-bound at 1.15-1.19pp over the last 10 readings, with the latest print (1.18) right at the middle of that recent cluster and near the top of its own short-term wobble. Longer-term momentum shows mild flattening pressure (63d change -0.22pp) but the very short-term change (21d -0.05pp) is small, and the last few prints (1.16, 1.18, 1.17, 1.18) show no clear directional trend, just noise. The spread sits only 0.03pp above its 12-month low and in just the 5th percentile of its 12-month range (slope_range_position 0.05), indicating the curve is near its most flattened point of the past year — but it isn't breaking further to new lows currently, it's stabilizing there. The policy language shift (from a hike to a "maintain" statement) reduces near-term front-end upward pressure, which could allow the curve to hold or steepen slightly rather than flatten further. With no fresh hiking signal and the spread hovering near-term without a fresh breakdown, I expect the spread to stay essentially unchanged into month-end, perhaps with a very slight steepening bias given the pause language, but not enough to call a strong directional move.
view.falsifier: A break below 1.15pp (new 12-month low) or a sustained move above 1.20pp would confirm renewed flattening or steepening respectively.

missing_inputs.why: Would clarify if broader tightening/loosening pressures are pushing long-end yields independent of Fed statement language.
Would show if long-end richness/cheapness is driving the 10s10y move rather than pure policy expectations.

raw_response: {"report": "The 2s10s spread has continued grinding lower, now at 0.96pp \u2014 a fresh 12-month low, sitting at the very bottom of its range (slope_range_position 0.00). My prior flat call was wrong: the trend that was already visible (slope_change_63d -0.34, slope_change_252d -0.41) has persisted into a new low rather than stabilizing. The last two prints (0.96, 0.96) show the pace of flattening decelerating somewhat versus the sharper drops earlier in the series, but there's no sign of reversal \u2014 no bounce, no steepening impulse. The 21-day change (-0.21pp) confirms the flattening is still active over the past month, not just a stale historical drift. Policy language shows the Fed moving from a hike to a hold, describing similarly moderate growth and gradual policy \u2014 this doesn't obviously argue for either curve direction from language alone, but the front end (dgs2 +0.78%) still reflects tightening expectations pressing against the back end (dgs10 +1.74%), keeping the spread compressed near cycle lows. With the spread pinned at the 12-month low and the multi-horizon momentum uniformly negative, the balance of evidence favors continued flattening or at best stabilization at a low level, not a rebound.", "key_evidence": "slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_12m_low, slope_range_position, slope_above_12m_low", "falsifier": "A rise back above ~1.05-1.10pp (clear break from the 12-month low) would signal steepening resumption; continued prints at/below 0.96 confirms flattening.", "missing_inputs": [{"driver": "curve_slope", "why": "More recent daily prints beyond the last observation would confirm whether the flattening pace is truly decelerating or resuming."}], "direction": "down", "conviction": 0.45}

view.reasoning: The 2s10s spread has continued grinding lower, now at 0.96pp — a fresh 12-month low, sitting at the very bottom of its range (slope_range_position 0.00). My prior flat call was wrong: the trend that was already visible (slope_change_63d -0.34, slope_change_252d -0.41) has persisted into a new low rather than stabilizing. The last two prints (0.96, 0.96) show the pace of flattening decelerating somewhat versus the sharper drops earlier in the series, but there's no sign of reversal — no bounce, no steepening impulse. The 21-day change (-0.21pp) confirms the flattening is still active over the past month, not just a stale historical drift. Policy language shows the Fed moving from a hike to a hold, describing similarly moderate growth and gradual policy — this doesn't obviously argue for either curve direction from language alone, but the front end (dgs2 +0.78%) still reflects tightening expectations pressing against the back end (dgs10 +1.74%), keeping the spread compressed near cycle lows. With the spread pinned at the 12-month low and the multi-horizon momentum uniformly negative, the balance of evidence favors continued flattening or at best stabilization at a low level, not a rebound.

My prior flat call was wrong: the trend that was already visible (slope_change_63d -0.34, slope_change_252d -0.41) has persisted into a new low rather than stabilizing. Policy language on this driver — unchanged context
  · The Committee expects that, with gradual adjustments in the stance of monetary policy, economic activity will expand at a moderate pace and labor market conditions will strengthen somewhat further.

  · Voting for the FOMC monetary policy action were: Janet L.

added: [date] Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement For release at [time] Information received since the Federal Open Market Committee met in September indicates that the labor market has continued to strengthen and growth of economic activity has picked up from the modest pace seen in the first half of this year.

Inflation has increased somewhat since earlier this year but is still below the Committee's 2 percent longer-run objective, partly reflecting earlier declines in energy prices and in prices of non-energy imports.

The Committee judges that the case for an increase in the federal funds rate has continued to strengthen but decided, for the time being, to wait for some further evidence of continued progress toward its objectives.
Mester, each of whom preferred at this meeting to raise the target range for the federal funds rate to 1/2 to 3/4 percent.
removed: [date] Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement For release at [time] Information received since the Federal Open Market Committee met in July indicates that the labor market has continued to strengthen and growth of economic activity has picked up from the modest pace seen in the first half of this year.
Inflation has continued to run below the Committee's 2 percent longer-run objective, partly reflecting earlier declines in energy prices and in prices of non-energy imports.

The Committee judges that the case for an increase in the federal funds rate has strengthened but decided, for the time being, to wait for further evidence of continued progress toward its objectives.
Mester, and Eric Rosengren, each of whom preferred at this meeting to raise the target range for the federal funds rate to 1/2 to 3/4 percent.
unchanged: The Committee expects that, with gradual adjustments in the stance of monetary policy, economic activity will expand at a moderate pace and labor market conditions will strengthen somewhat further.
Against this backdrop, the Committee decided to maintain the target range for the federal funds rate at 1/4 to 1/2 percent.
The stance of monetary policy remains accommodative, thereby supporting further improvement in labor market conditions and a return to 2 percent inflation.

In determining the timing and size of future adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the Committee will assess realized and expected economic conditions relative to its objectives of maximum employment and 2 percent inflation.

The Committee expects that economic conditions will evolve in a manner that will warrant only gradual increases in the federal funds rate; the federal funds rate is likely to remain, for some time, below levels that are expected to prevail in the longer run.
However, the actual path of the federal funds rate will depend on the economic outlook as informed by incoming data.

The Committee is maintaining its existing policy of reinvesting principal payments from its holdings of agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities in agency mortgage-backed securities and of rolling over maturing Treasury securities at auction, and it anticipates doing so until normalization of the level of the federal funds rate is well under way.
This policy, by keeping the Committee's holdings of longer-term securities at sizable levels, should help maintain accommodative financial conditions.
Voting for the FOMC monetary policy action were: Janet L.

Last release I called for further flattening (down) with 0.55 conviction, expecting the trend near the 12-month low to persist. The new prints show the opposite in the very short term: the last two observations (0.60, 0.58) rebounded from the recent low of 0.53, and slope_change_21d is now +0.07pp, reversing the negative 63d (-0.21) and 252d (-0.69) trends. That was my stated falsifier — a rebound back above 0.55-0.58 — and it has occurred, so my prior down call was wrong for the most recent month.

That said, the spread remains deeply compressed: slope_range_position of 0.10 shows the curve is still near the bottom decile of its 12-month range, and slope_distance_from_flat at just 0.58pp confirms the curve remains historically flat with little cushion before inversion. The 21d bounce is modest and could easily be noise within a still-flattening regime, especially as the longer-horizon trends (63d, 252d) remain firmly negative. The 10s30s slope at 0.23 also suggests limited long-end steepening pressure.

Given the conflicting signals — a genuine but small near-term uptick against a dominant multi-quarter flattening trend and a curve sitting near cycle lows — I don't have conviction for a continued strong down move, but the balance of evidence doesn't support calling steepening either. I'll set this closer to flat, with a slight lean toward down given the still-negative longer-term momentum.

raw_response: {"report": "The 2s10s spread has settled around 1.04-1.07 in the last three readings, down from the 1.16-1.25 range earlier in the series. My prior call for steepening (up) was wrong: the spread broke below the 1.15 threshold I flagged as a flattening signal, and has held there for three consecutive readings rather than bouncing back. The 21-day change is -0.18pp (recent flattening), even though the 63-day change is roughly flat (+0.02pp) and the 252-day change is still positive (+0.44pp), showing the broader steepening trend has stalled and reversed short-term. Both 2s and 10s yields have risen (dgs2 +0.48%, dgs10 +1.55%), with the front end selling off less but the curve compressing, consistent with the new policy language flagging that tapering \"may soon be warranted\" \u2014 a hawkish shift that tends to pull front-end yields up faster than the long end, flattening the curve. Range position is mid-pack (0.45), so this isn't extreme inversion territory, but the recent trend and the policy signal both point the same direction. Given the stabilization at 1.04-1.07 for three readings and the tapering signal working against further steepening, I lean toward continued flattening or at best a flat/rangebound spread near current lows.", "key_evidence": ["slope_2s10s (last 3 obs: 1.04, 1.07, 1.07)", "slope_change_21d", "slope_change_63d", "slope_change_252d", "slope_range_position", "dgs2", "dgs10"], "falsifier": "A rebound above ~1.15 sustained over 2+ readings would signal renewed steepening; further sustained drift below 1.00 would confirm accelerating flattening.", "missing_inputs": [{"driver": "inflation_expectations", "why": "Shifts in long-run inflation expectations could explain whether long-end yields stabilize or keep lagging front end."}, {"driver": "financial_conditions", "why": "Would clarify how much of front-end rise reflects tightening expectations versus other flows."}], "direction": "flat", "conviction": 0.35}

view.reasoning: The 2s10s spread has settled around 1.04-1.07 in the last three readings, down from the 1.16-1.25 range earlier in the series. My prior call for steepening (up) was wrong: the spread broke below the 1.15 threshold I flagged as a flattening signal, and has held there for three consecutive readings rather than bouncing back. The 21-day change is -0.18pp (recent flattening), even though the 63-day change is roughly flat (+0.02pp) and the 252-day change is still positive (+0.44pp), showing the broader steepening trend has stalled and reversed short-term. Both 2s and 10s yields have risen (dgs2 +0.48%, dgs10 +1.55%), with the front end selling off less but the curve compressing, consistent with the new policy language flagging that tapering "may soon be warranted" — a hawkish shift that tends to pull front-end yields up faster than the long end, flattening the curve. Range position is mid-pack (0.45), so this isn't extreme inversion territory, but the recent trend and the policy signal both point the same direction. Given the stabilization at 1.04-1.07 for three readings and the tapering signal working against further steepening, I lean toward continued flattening or at best a flat/rangebound spread near current lows.
